description: Evidence-based investment research and pattern recognition frameworks

primary_model.name: Peter Lynch Investment Methodology
primary_model.source: Peter Lynch - Fidelity Magellan Fund Manager
primary_model.citation: One Up on Wall Street (1989); Beating the Street (1993)
primary_model.track_record: 29.2% average annual return 1977-1990, nearly doubling S&P 500
primary_model.core_insight: Average investors can pick winning stocks by investing in what they know and doing fundamental research. But knowing a company's products isn't enough - you must understand the financials.

six_stock_categories.characteristics: Large, established companies with steady growth
Small, aggressive companies. Highest reward AND risk.
six_stock_categories.strategy: Find ones with sustainable growth. Can be 10-baggers.
six_stock_categories.warning: Fast growth can't last forever. Watch for slowing.

six_stock_categories.characteristics: Revenues and profits rise/fall with economic cycles
six_stock_categories.strategy: Buy at the bottom of the cycle, sell at the top

six_stock_categories.characteristics: Companies recovering from crisis or near-bankruptcy
six_stock_categories.strategy: High risk, high reward if recovery succeeds
six_stock_categories.key_question: Is the company actually fixing the problem?

six_stock_categories.characteristics: Company owns valuable assets not reflected in stock price
six_stock_categories.strategy: Find hidden value that Wall Street is missing

PEG_ratio.interpretation: PEG < 1 = potentially undervalued. PEG = 1 = fairly valued. PEG > 1 = potentially overvalued
PEG_ratio.lynch_insight: A company growing at 20% with P/E of 20 is fairly valued. Same P/E with 10% growth is expensive.

debt_to_equity.preference: Lower is better, especially for cyclicals and turnarounds

earnings_growth.look_for: Consistent earnings growth, not one-time spikes

free_cash_flow.definition: Cash left after business operations and capital expenditures
free_cash_flow.insight: Cash is harder to manipulate than earnings

behavioral_finance_model.name: Behavioral Economics in Investing
behavioral_finance_model.core_insight: Investors are not rational. Understanding biases helps avoid them.

key_biases.description: Losses feel ~2x as painful as equivalent gains feel good
key_biases.manifestation: Holding losers too long, selling winners too early
key_biases.coaching_response: Ask: If you didn't own this stock, would you buy it today?

key_biases.description: Seeking information that confirms existing beliefs
key_biases.manifestation: Only reading bullish analysis on stocks you own
key_biases.coaching_response: Actively seek the bear case. What could go wrong?

key_biases.manifestation: Assuming recent trends will continue forever
key_biases.coaching_response: Zoom out. How does this look over 5-10 years?

key_biases.manifestation: Buying at tops (everyone's buying!), selling at bottoms (everyone's selling!)
key_biases.lynch_quote: If you buy the same stocks as everyone else, you'll get the same results as everyone else

key_biases.coaching_response: What's your edge? If none, maybe index.

invest_in_what_you_know.definition: Use your everyday experience to spot investment opportunities
invest_in_what_you_know.caveat: Liking a product is NOT enough. You must research the financials.
process: Notice companies you encounter in daily life
Ask: Is this company gaining or losing customers?
Only invest after fundamental analysis confirms the story

the_story.definition: Understanding WHY a company will succeed
the_story.two_minute_monologue: If you can't explain why you own a stock in two minutes, you shouldn't own it

institutional_neglect.insight: Bargains are often found where Wall Street isn't looking
institutional_neglect.opportunity: Less competition for research = more potential mispricing

pattern_recognition.peter_insight: Turn over more rocks. Look at more companies. The person who turns over the most rocks wins.
cross_domain_patterns: Spending patterns that indicate product demand
Job postings that indicate company expansion
Industry trends that lift multiple stocks

qualitative_factors: Understandable business model (can you explain it?)

cross_domain_insights.spending_to_stock: What are people buying? Is there a public company behind it?
cross_domain_insights.habits_to_stock: What products support healthy habits? Growing markets.
cross_domain_insights.career_to_stock: What industries are hiring? Growing demand.
cross_domain_insights.life_events_to_stock: Weddings, babies, houses - all drive spending to specific companies

coaching_principles.turn_over_rocks: Research volume matters. Look at many to find few.
coaching_principles.know_what_you_own: If you can't explain it, don't own it
coaching_principles.long_term_wins: Time in the market beats timing the market
coaching_principles.ignore_the_noise: Short-term volatility is irrelevant to long-term investors
coaching_principles.failure_is_data: Even Lynch was wrong 40% of the time. The key is sizing winners bigger.
